reasoning: The code pattern exists exactly as described: Plugin_Upgrader::plugin_info() returns a relative plugin path (e.g., 'content-hub-embed/content-embed.php') and it is included without stripping in the wp_send_json_success() payload on line 102. However, the finding fails on meaningful security impact for three reasons: (1) Auth gate is strict — both current_user_can('install_plugins') and wp_verify_nonce() must pass (line 35), restricting this exclusively to WordPress administrators with full admin access; (2) The disclosed value is a relative plugin path, not an absolute server filesystem path — it does not reveal /var/www/html or any OS-level directory layout; (3) The plugin name is already derivable from the hardcoded constant CONTENT_EMBED_LINK = 'https://api.hubapi.com/content-embed/v1/plugin/download/content-hub-embed.zip' in the same file, making it public knowledge. There is no attacker-controlled input; $plugin_info comes entirely from the installed plugin's own header data. Stripping with basename() would actually break activate_plugin($plugin_info) on line 107. No meaningful attack path is enabled by this disclosure to a user who already holds install_plugins capability.
